text: Today, I will present our work titled "Detecting Safety Violations Across Many Agent Traces."
This research was conducted by Adam Stein, Davis Brown, Hamed Hassani, Mayur Naik, and Eric Wong.
We are with the University of Pennsylvania, and the presentation is based on our June 2026 preprint.
AI safety auditing has traditionally focused on single agent traces, as shown in the first bullet.
However, in practical deployments, violations appear distributed across multiple traces.
Existing approaches often miss failures that require joint analysis, as indicated in the third point.
We face challenges such as rare failures, adversarial disguises, and scale, detailed in the challenge section.
These issues motivate new approaches for detecting cross-trace safety violations.
This slide illustrates a motivating example: distributed ransomware detection.
The figure shows how benign-looking traces, when combined, violate the safety property against ransomware.
A small set of traces, called the violating witness set W, enables a successful ransomware attack.
Prior research includes single-trace monitoring for sabotage and deception detection.
Automated audits leverage red teaming and LLM-based evaluations.
Corpus-scale analysis enables human exploration and clustering of traces at scale.
However, most methods miss hyperproperty violations and formal methods struggle at scale and with language.
Our goal is to audit a repository of agent traces for violation of a natural-language property φ.
Our method finds sets, or witnesses, of traces that collectively form a violation.
The process includes embedding traces, clustering them, constructing prompts, and searching for violating sets.
This framework enables joint analysis to detect failures missed by per-trace monitoring.
We define the setup with a repository R of traces and a forbidden safety property φ in natural language.
A cross-trace violation occurs if a subset W of traces jointly violates φ.
The objective is to identify such witness sets or conclude no violation exists.
Key challenges include visibility only across traces, a majority of benign traces, and adversarial disguises.
